1:
The chapter that opens the Chronicler's long register opens where all Scripture's pedigree begins, with the first man himself. The reformed John Gill observes that these first four verses agree exactly with the account of the antediluvian patriarchs in Gen. 5, and notes the scribal curiosity that the first letter of Adam's name is written larger than usual, a memorial, as Buxtorf observed, of the first and only man, from whom mankind had its beginning and whose history the author had undertaken to write. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the book among the Ketuvim or hagiography, one of the five later historical books, and remark that its genealogies reach back beyond the settled history all the way to Adam, the father of the human race.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the whole paragraph a single arch: it has Adam for its first word and Abraham for its last, and between the creation of the one and the birth of the other lay some two thousand years, almost half of which Adam himself lived. Adam, he says, was the common father of our flesh, Abraham the common father of the faithful; by the breach the former made of the covenant of innocency we were all made miserable, and by the covenant of grace made with the latter we all may be made happy. We are by nature the seed of Adam, branches of the wild olive; let us see to it that by faith we become the seed of Abraham (Rom. 4:11-12), grafted into the good olive. The first four verses and the last four, linked together by Shem, contain the sacred line of Christ from Adam to Abraham, and are taken up in his pedigree in Luke 3:34-38, the order there ascending as here it descends. With Bishop Patrick, Henry calls it the peculiar glory of the Jewish nation that they alone could derive their pedigree from the first man God created, while other nations abused themselves with fabulous originals, the Arcadians fancying they were before the moon, the Thessalians that they sprang from stones, the Athenians that they grew out of the earth.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the book's frame: the two books of Chronicles were anciently one; the Hebrew title means the words of the days, that is, the journals, while the Septuagint called them Paralipomenon, the things omitted or left over. He judges Ezra the probable author, and notes that of Adam's children no notice is taken of Cain or Abel, since one line only, from Adam to Noah, was intended, and all their posterity had perished in the deluge. Clarke has no patience with those who find a mystery hidden in every name, mocking the profound illustrators who would draw from the first verse alone a full scheme of man's fall and restoration. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds that Adam is here treated as a proper name, where in Gen. 5:1-5 it is an appellative; he sets the list beside the Chaldean tradition of ten antediluvian kings, from Alorus to Xisuthrus the hero of the Flood, whose reigns were reckoned at 432,000 years, and finds the sober monotheism of the Hebrew record the more original. Against those who would dissolve these names into solar myths, he insists that Sheth and Enosh belong to no mythology at all. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley notes only that for brevity's sake the historian gives the bare names, the rest being easily understood out of the former books.
5:
The first stream to be traced is Japheth's, and the witnesses agree that the historian begins here precisely because these were the peoples furthest from the covenant. The reformed John Gill notes that the genealogy of Noah's sons after the flood opens with Japheth the elder, then passes to Ham the younger, and sets Shem last of all, out of the order of birth, because from Shem in the line of Heber sprang Abraham, the ancestor of the Jewish nation and of the Messiah, for whose sake the whole genealogy is given. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the arrangement the same way: the historian, repeating the replenishing of the earth, begins with those who were strangers to the church, the sons of Japheth, who peopled Europe, of whom he says little because the Jews had hitherto had little or no dealings with them, and then hastens toward the line of Abraham.

The seven sons named, Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras, are the roots of the northern and western nations, the isles of the Gentiles. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws in the Chaldee paraphrase of Rabbi Joseph, which he cites throughout as the Targum, and reports its attempt to name their countries, Africa, Germany, Media, Macedonia, Bithynia, Maesia, and Thrace, with a second copy differing in its readings, a reminder of how the old interpreters strove to fasten each ancient name to a known land. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives them the same short account, since with these western families the people of God had as yet little to do.

The witnesses find the deepest meaning not in the individual names but in the direction the historian faces. Wesley notes that the great promise of the Messiah was transmitted from Adam to Seth, from Seth to Shem, from Shem to Eber, and so to the Hebrew nation, which was entrusted above all nations with that sacred treasure until the promise was performed; and it is toward that line, and away from these, that the whole chapter is bending. Japheth's sons are set down first, then, only to be set aside, their brief notice marking the outer edge of the world that the covenant would one day reach, but from which, for now, the Chronicler turns to follow the narrower thread of promise.
28:
The sons of Abraham are named, and with them the register reaches the fountainhead of the whole book: Isaac and Ishmael. The reformed John Gill notes that Abraham, the famous ancestor of the Jews, is set forth here with his firstborn Ishmael and his posterity, his sons by Keturah, and Isaac and his sons, the whole account agreeing throughout with the record in Gen. 25. Isaac is named after Ishmael in the Hebrew order of birth, yet the witnesses know at once which of the two the historian means to keep.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the verse a meditation on election. All nations but the seed of Abraham, he says, are now shaken off from this genealogy; the Lord's portion is his people, of whom he keeps a particular account and knows by name, while those who are strangers to him he beholds afar off. Yet Henry is careful to guard the truth from abuse: we are not to conclude that no one of any other nation found favour with God, for it was true, before Peter perceived it, that in every nation he that feareth God and worketh righteousness is accepted; multitudes will be brought to heaven out of all nations (Rev. 7:9), and many a good soul outside the covenant of peculiarity had his name in the book of life. Israel was a chosen nation, elect in type, but the election of a people to outward privilege did not shut up the mercy of God to them alone.

Of Ishmael the witnesses say what the covenant said. Henry marks him as the son of the bondwoman, cast out and not to be heir with the child of promise, his case representing that of the unbelieving Jews who were afterward rejected (Gal. 4:22); and yet his twelve sons are named in the verses that follow to show the performance of God's word to Abraham, that for his sake Ishmael too should become a great nation and beget twelve princes (Gen. 17:20). The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley echoes the same double note, that all nations but the seed of Abraham are here shaken off, yet without concluding that no particular person of any other nation found favour with God, for many, very many, whose names were in the book of life did not spring from the loins of Abraham. So the witnesses make this pivotal verse both a narrowing and a widening: the line of the register contracts to Isaac, but the mercy of God is not confined to the line.
35:
Turning to the rejected brother first, the historian numbers the sons of Esau: Eliphaz, Reuel, and Jeush, and Jaalam, and Korah. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that this whole account of Esau and of Seir is an extract from Gen. 36, and that the five sons here named are the same who, according to that chapter, were born to Esau of his three wives in the land of Canaan. They mark one small spelling difference of the kind that runs through the parallel: the name written Jeush here is only another form of the reading that stands in Gen. 36:5, a variation in the vowels rather than a different man.

The reformed John Gill, opening the same list, is chiefly concerned to forestall a confusion that the next verse will raise. Among the descendants soon to be named is Timna, and Gill warns in advance that Timna is not the name of one of Esau's grandsons but of the concubine of Eliphaz, the mother of Amalek; so the Arabic version reads, and so the Alexandrian copy of the Greek, and the reader is not to mistake a woman's name for a man's. Gill thus prepares the ground for the difficulty that will divide the witnesses at the following verse.

The witnesses read the opening of Esau's genealogy as the beginning of a long, faithful parenthesis. The Chronicler, having reached Isaac's two sons, deals with the elder and rejected line before the younger and chosen, exactly as he had dealt with Ishmael and Keturah before Isaac. Keil and Delitzsch's note that the whole is copied from the law reminds the reader that nothing here is fresh invention; the historian is preserving, name by name, the record already given of Edom. And Gill's early caution about Timna signals that even in so borrowed a list the commentators will attend closely to each name, watching for the points, whether of spelling or of identity, at which the Chronicler's abridgment might mislead an incautious eye. The five sons of Esau stand, then, as the head of the Edomite nation, set down that the word of God concerning Esau, too, might be seen fulfilled.
38:
The register now takes in a line that was not Esau's at all, the sons of Seir, Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. The reformed John Gill explains why these aliens are here: Seir and his posterity were not of the race of Esau, but are mentioned because they were a family into which Esau and a son of his married, and whose possessions Esau and his people came to hold. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees, calling Seir one of another nation, a prince of the Horites, whose genealogy is set down because of the affinity contracted between his posterity and Esau's; and those of the old inhabitants who were not united and incorporated with the newcomers, he notes, were in time destroyed by them, as the law records (Deut. 2:12).

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set out the history behind the list. When Esau with his descendants had settled in Mount Seir, they subdued by degrees the aboriginal inhabitants of the land and became fused with them into one people; and for this reason the older record notes the tribal princes of the Seirites alongside those of Esau, as the Chronicler does here. They reckon the whole population of Seir, after Esau's immigration, at twenty tribes under a like number of chiefs, thirteen of them Edomite, of the family of Esau, and seven Seirite, the sons of Seir named in this verse, whom the law calls Horites, that is, Troglodytes or cave-dwellers, the aborigines of the land.

The witnesses thus read the verse as the record of a people absorbed. The Horite Seir and his seven sons stand in the sacred register not for their own greatness but because Esau's house married into them, dispossessed them, and took their mountain land, so that the two stocks, conqueror and conquered, are numbered together as the population of Edom. Gill and Wesley and Keil and Delitzsch converge on this: the genealogy of Seir is Edom's title-deed, showing by what affinity and what conquest Esau came to dwell in the land promised him, while the older inhabitants, save those blended into his tribes, passed away. It is a small picture of the way of the nations, one people rising upon the ruins of another; and it is set here, the witnesses feel, to complete the account of Esau's settlement before the historian counts the kings who would reign in that land.
43:
The chapter turns from clans to crowns, and the witnesses feel the weight of the notice: these are the kings that reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the children of Israel. The reformed John Gill marks that the land took its name from Esau, who was called Edom, and that from here to the chapter's end an account is given of the kings and dukes of Edom, in the same order as in the older record. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dwells on the significance of the dating clause. Edom, as the older people, earlier established in its permanent home, was naturally a stage beyond Israel in political development, already ruled by kings while Israel was still a wandering family; and this notice, brief as it is, he judges very fittingly inserted here at the head of a book that is itself an introduction to the history of the kings of the house of David, the shadow-monarchy of Edom set before the true kingdom that was to come.

Over the first king, Bela the son of Beor, the witnesses divide in a smaller matter. Ellicott observes that the name is curiously like Balaam the son of Beor, the two words being essentially similar in Hebrew, the closing consonant of the longer name possibly a mere formative addition; and he wonders whether the prophet whose strange story is read in the law might, like Isaiah, have been of royal extraction, though he offers as an alternative that Balaam may rather mean Bel is a kinsman. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the Targum going much further, which flatly identifies Bela with Balaam the impious son of Beor, and even with Laban the Syrian, making him a confederate of Esau's sons against Jacob who afterward reigned in Sodom. So the question stands between a cautious philological resemblance and a bold traditional identification: is the Edomite Bela the same man as the prophet Balaam, or only a namesake?

On the city the two agree in substance. Ellicott renders Dinhabah as doom-giving, the place where the king gave judgment (1 Sam. 8:5); the Targum, as Clarke gives it, offers a more moralizing gloss on the name. The witnesses set the whole verse, with its message once carried to a king of Edom in the wilderness (Num. 20:14-21), as the opening of a deliberate contrast: Edom had its line of kings before Israel had any, yet those kings, as the following verses show, did not pass their thrones from father to son. The elder nation ran ahead into monarchy, but it was over the younger, in the house of David, that God would raise the enduring throne toward which the Chronicler's whole book is written.
2:
The line runs on through the middle antediluvian fathers, Kenan, Mahalaleel, and Jered, and the witnesses use this quiet stretch to explain the Chronicler's method rather than to dwell on the men. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that these verses simply carry the descent from Adam down to Noah and his three sons, and that the writer, drawing on the older books, has deliberately stripped the record to its names, omitting the introductory and concluding remarks of his source and the narrative notices that once accompanied it. Where Gen. 5 paused over each patriarch's years and death, the Chronicler keeps only the succession, content that his readers know the fuller story already.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had gathered these same names into his single sweep from Adam to Abraham, reminding us that this first stretch, with the four verses that answer to it further down, holds the sacred line of Christ, the pedigree no other nation could show. The reformed John Gill, likewise, treats the four opening verses as one block agreeing throughout with the older genealogy, so that Kenan, Mahalaleel, and Jered are not new figures to be described but links in a chain already forged in the law. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley states the principle plainly: Adam begat Sheth, and so through all the following particulars; for brevity's sake the writer mentions their names only, and the rest is easily understood out of the former books.

The witnesses thus make of this bare verse a lesson in how a genealogy preaches. Ten generations are compressed from the first man to the flood, each name a life that filled its span and passed it on, and the whole set down not for antiquarian curiosity but to keep unbroken the thread that runs to the promised Seed. Keil and Delitzsch note that the very sparseness is intentional and instructive: the Chronicler is not writing a chronicle of the antediluvian world but tracing a covenant, and every name he keeps is kept because it stands in the line of promise. Henry's reflection hovers over the reading, that these patriarchs had their day and made way for those who came after, so that a list of the long-dead becomes a meditation on the God who carries his purpose forward through generation after generation, from Kenan and Jered down to the Christ in whom the line at last finds its meaning.
6:
The line of Japheth is carried one generation further in the sons of Gomer, Ashchenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah, and here the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch enter their careful account of how the Chronicler's copy differs from the older text. The whole section, they note, is an abridgment of the Table of Nations, from which the author has omitted not only the framing remarks but the historical notices, the founding of the kingdom in Babel and the settling of the several families in their dwelling-places. What remains diverges from Gen. 10 in two ways, partly by orthography and partly through errors of transcription, and this verse furnishes a plain instance: the name written Riphath in Gen. 10:3 appears here as Diphath, the letter that opens the word having been altered in the copying.

Such variation, the witnesses insist, is no ground for stumbling. Keil and Delitzsch treat these divergences soberly as the ordinary marks of hand-copied texts, sometimes a mere difference of spelling, sometimes a letter transposed or exchanged, and they are content in many cases to leave open which form is the original. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke again brings in the Targum, which for this verse ventures the countries of Gomer's sons, naming Asia, Persia, and Barbary, one more attempt of the old paraphrasts to give the shadowy names a place on the map of the known world.

Behind the philology the witnesses keep the larger truth that governs the whole register. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already reminded us that at the first the originals of the respective nations were known, but that by the enlargement of commerce and dominion, the transplanting of colonies, and the carrying away of captives, the nations have since become so mingled that no one people descends entire from any single one of these fountains. Togarmah and his brothers stand, then, not as tidy modern nations but as the far springs of peoples long since flowed together, and the small quarrel over whether the name be Riphath or Diphath only underlines how much of that ancient world has passed beyond certain recovery, leaving the covenant line, which the historian guards so carefully, as the one thread traced without a break.
25:
The resumed line runs swiftly through the post-flood fathers, Eber, Peleg, Reu, and on. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the succession without a break from Eber through Peleg, Reu, Serug, Nahor, and Terah to Abram, and note the plain purpose of the sequence: it is the ten-linked chain that carries the covenant from Shem to Abraham, answering to the ten generations that had carried it from Adam to Noah. Here the genealogy is at its most rapid, a single name to a generation, because the historian is now simply spanning the distance from the flood to the patriarch with whom his real interest lies.

The witnesses feel the quiet momentum of the list, for every name in it is a step nearer the promise. Peleg, in whose days the earth was divided, and Reu and Serug after him, are not lingered over; they are the bridge between the scattering of the nations and the calling of the one man through whom the nations would be blessed. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's governing thought presides here too, that the register hastens to the line of Abraham, keeping only the family of Arphaxad, from whom Christ was to come, and letting all else fall away.

Keil and Delitzsch mark that this stretch closes upon its goal in the great name toward which the whole chapter has been bending, for the line reaches Terah and then Abram, of whom the text will say that this is the same as Abraham. The witnesses read the ten generations as a single sustained act of narrowing: out of all the peoples spread abroad after the flood, the Chronicler follows one thread, unremarkable name by unremarkable name, until it arrives at the man in whom God would gather up all his promises. The very brevity is eloquent. Where the nations were spread wide and counted once, the covenant line is drawn thin and straight, each father named only to hand the promise on, so that the reader is brought, almost before he is aware, from the confusion of Babel to the threshold of the call of Abraham.
29:
Before following Isaac, the historian pays out the promise made to Ishmael, numbering the twelve princes of his house: These are their generations; the firstborn of Ishmael, Nebaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, and Mibsam. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott glosses the opening formula, that the word rendered generations means their genealogy or register of births, the record of a man's posterity, and answers to the phrase that stands at the head of Matt. 1:1, the book of the generation. He notes that the twelve sons or tribes of Ishmael are given first, in an extract from the older register, and that they are more than names on a page: Nebaioth is the Nabateans of Arabia Petraea, and Kedar the Cedrei of the classical writers, the two set together by the prophet as the flocks of the desert (Isa. 60:7); Adbeel he identifies with a tribe named in the Assyrian records as paying tribute to Tiglath-pileser, settled toward the Egyptian border.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm that these names of Ishmael's generations answer to those in Gen. 25:12-15 and have been explained there, the Chronicler simply taking them over from the law. The witnesses thus read the roll not as a genealogical curiosity but as the visible discharge of a divine word: the twelve princes God had promised Abraham for Ishmael's sake are here set down by name, and Ellicott's identifications show them still traceable in the desert tribes and even in the tribute-lists of Assyrian kings, the Nabateans and the Kedarenes and their neighbours.

The witnesses feel the fitness of this fulfillment standing just before the turn to Isaac. God had said that Ishmael, though not the heir of promise, should be made a great nation and beget twelve princes, and the historian, faithful to the record, pauses to count them out, Nebaioth the firstborn at their head. It is the same God who keeps his lesser word to the son of the bondwoman and his greater word to the son of promise; and the reader, seeing the twelve Ishmaelite tribes named and then set aside, is prepared for the register to leave this fulfilled but rejected line and hold once more to Isaac, in whom the seed was to be called.
30:
The list of Ishmael's princes runs on through Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, and Tema, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lodges several of them firmly in the geography of the northern Arabian desert. Dumah he connects with the place the prophet names in his burden (Isa. 21:11), a spot still known as Duma the Rocky on the borders of the Syrian desert and Arabia; Tema he sets in the north of the Arabian waste, a name preserved in the Assyrian records, and warns that the Greek translators confused it with Teman, which belongs rather to Edom. Of Hadad he remarks only that this is the right reading of the name, correcting a common corruption.

The witnesses value the verse for the way it anchors the promise in real places. These are not shadowy names but the desert tribes and oasis-towns of Arabia, some of them, as Ellicott shows, still to be found on the map or in the tribute-lists of the great kings, others named again by Israel's prophets. The twelve princes God pledged to Abraham for Ishmael's sake spread out, in this line, into the wilderness south and east of Canaan, a great nation in the fastnesses of the desert, exactly as the word had said. Ellicott's patient linking of Dumah and Tema to known oases and to the annals of the Assyrian kings turns the bare roll into evidence, the promise made visible in the very tribes and towns that still bore these names long centuries after Ishmael.

What the commentators do not do is dwell on these names beyond their bare identification, and the witnesses take the restraint as itself instructive. The Chronicler is discharging a debt of accuracy to the promise, not opening a history of the Ishmaelite tribes; he names Mishma and Dumah and Tema because they were among the twelve, and then moves on. So the verse sits in the register as a faithful notice of a fulfilled but collateral line, the desert peoples counted and honoured with a place in the sacred record, yet plainly outside the narrowing channel through which the historian is bending toward Isaac and the covenant seed. Ellicott's careful geography serves that larger reading, showing a promise kept in the dust of Arabia while the true line of blessing runs elsewhere.
36:
The grandsons of Esau by Eliphaz are numbered, Teman, Omar, Zephi, Gatam, and Kenaz, and then two further names, Timna and Amalek, and it is over these last that the witnesses genuinely divide. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse against Gen. 36:11-12, where Eliphaz has five sons and where Timna is expressly a concubine of Eliphaz who bore him Amalek. The Chronicler's stringing of Timna and Amalek onto the list of sons is therefore, they judge, merely an abbreviation, which the author might allow himself because his readers knew the fuller account from the law; the very form of the name Timna, a feminine formation, guards against the notion that she was a son, and they expressly reject the misinterpretation that would make Timna one of the men. So read, Esau had through Eliphaz six grandsons, and Amalek was born of the concubine.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley takes the other road. He grants that there was indeed a Timna who was the concubine of Eliphaz, named in Gen. 36:12, but holds that this Timna, listed here among the sons, was one of Eliphaz's sons, though called by the same name; for there are, he observes, some names common both to men and women in Hebrew as in other tongues. On Wesley's reading the Chronicler has not abbreviated at all but recorded a distinct male Timna alongside the female one known from Genesis. The disagreement is real: was Timna in this verse the concubine, elliptically listed, or a son who happened to share her name?

The reformed John Gill stands with Keil and Delitzsch, having already declared at the previous verse that Timna is the concubine of Eliphaz and mother of Amalek, on the authority of the Arabic and Alexandrian readings. Keil and Delitzsch add a smaller textual note on which all agree, that the name Zephi here is only another form of the Zepho of the older text, one more of the slight orthographic shifts between the two registers. The witnesses thus leave the verse with a settled majority and a lone dissenter: Gill and the grammatical-historical school reading Timna as the concubine whom the Chronicler names in shorthand, and Wesley alone contending for a second Timna among Eliphaz's sons, a difference that turns wholly on how strictly the abridged list is to be pressed.
37:
The second branch of Esau's grandsons is given in the four sons of Reuel, Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch use the verse to complete a reckoning. Reuel was Esau's son by Bashemath, and his four sons answer to those named in Gen. 36:13; these four, added to the six grandsons through Eliphaz, make ten, and these ten, together with Esau's three sons by Aholibamah, Jeush, Jaalam, and Korah, are the founders of the thirteen tribes of the posterity of Esau. In the older record, they observe, these same thirteen are called the chiefs or phylarchs of the sons of Esau (Gen. 36:15), the heads from whom the Edomite clans descended.

Keil and Delitzsch press this arithmetic against a mistaken count they mean to correct. It is an error, they insist, to suppose that Esau's people were divided into twelve tribes, five from Eliphaz, four from Reuel, and three from Aholibamah, with Amalek loosely attached as the son of a concubine; neither the Chronicler nor the law knows anything of twelve tribes of Edom. Both books alike reckon thirteen grandsons of Esau, and thirteen the founders of the Edomite tribes, and the witnesses follow their careful numbering as the sober correction of a modern miscount.

The witnesses find in the tally more than a sum. That Esau's house should be built up into thirteen tribes, each with its chief, is the visible keeping of God's word that Esau too should become a nation, the elder brother made the father of a settled and ordered people even as he was passed by for the covenant. Keil and Delitzsch's precision, refusing to round the number down or to shuffle Amalek out of the count, honours the text's own witness that from the two sons Eliphaz and Reuel, and the three sons of Aholibamah, sprang the whole company of Edomite phylarchs. The reader, watching the rejected line spread into its thirteen clans, is prepared for the roll of Edomite dukes and kings that the chapter will presently unfold, and for the steady lesson that God is faithful even in the promises he makes to the branch that stands outside the line of blessing.
39:
The Horite line is traced into its next generation, the sons of Lotan, Hori and Homam, with the added note that Timna was Lotan's sister. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, handling the whole block of Seir's descendants together, observe that these names answer to those of the older record and have been explained there, the two lists corresponding except in a few unimportant points; the small differences of spelling, such as the form Homam bears here against its counterpart in Gen. 36:22, are of the ordinary kind that run through the parallel and disturb nothing of substance.

The witnesses take particular note of the sister named at the verse's close, for Timna the sister of Lotan is the very woman who, in the account of Esau's house, became the concubine of Eliphaz and the mother of Amalek. Her appearance here in the Horite genealogy, and there in the Edomite, quietly binds the two lines together: the aboriginal Seirites and the incoming sons of Esau are joined not only by conquest and neighbourhood but by marriage, a Horite woman passing into the family of Esau's firstborn. The commentators who had puzzled over Timna's name a few verses earlier find her placed here at her origin, a daughter of the old inhabitants of the land.

So the witnesses read the verse as one more strand in the weaving of Edom out of two peoples. The Chronicler, faithfully copying the law, keeps even the Horite clans and their women, because they were absorbed into the nation that sprang from Esau; and the naming of Timna as Lotan's sister shows the reader by what threads the fusion was accomplished. Keil and Delitzsch's calm treatment of the spelling variants, neither hiding them nor magnifying them, is of a piece with the whole method the witnesses have watched through the chapter: the text is received as it stands, its minor differences from the older register noted and let be, while the substance of the genealogy, the descent and intermarriage that made Edom a single people in Mount Seir, is preserved entire. The single detail of Timna, named here at her Horite origin and reappearing in Esau's house as the mother of Amalek, is enough to show how tightly the two peoples were knit, and why the Chronicler would not let fall even the sisters and daughters of the old inhabitants of the land.
44:
The succession of Edom's kings begins its march: when Bela was dead, Jobab the son of Zerah of Bozrah reigned in his stead. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, taking the list as a whole, note that these princes of Edom answer to those in Gen. 36:40-43, though the heading and the closing words in the older record are quite differently framed; the Chronicler has taken over the roll of kings while shaping its frame to his own purpose. The witnesses observe at once the strange character of this monarchy, plain from the very first succession: Jobab is not Bela's son but a man of another city, Bozrah, so that the crown passes not by descent but from one house and town to another, an elective or unsettled kingship rather than a dynasty.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gathers two traditions about the men. Of Bela he reports the Targum's tale that he was killed by Phinehas in the wilderness; and of Jobab the son of Zerah he weighs the old opinion, held by some, that this king was the same as Job, whose book stands in the canon. Clarke rejects the identification on the plain ground of the names: Job is written one way in the Hebrew and Jobab quite another, so that for all the likeness of sound there is no real sameness, and he refers the reader to his notes on Job and on the parallel place in the law.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as both a record and a caution against hasty identifications. The kingdom of Edom, they note, was of a kind unlike the settled thrones of the nations, its kings rising one after another out of different cities with no son succeeding his father, a monarchy without a house. And the guess that would make its second king the patriarch Job, tempting for the mere echo of the names, is set aside by Clarke's sober attention to the spelling, an instance of the whole chapter's discipline, that resemblance of sound is no proof of identity. Jobab of Bozrah stands, therefore, simply as the second in a line of Edomite kings whose very disorder of succession quietly points the contrast with the ordered and enduring throne that God was preparing in Israel.
52:
The roll of Edom's chiefs continues: duke Aholibamah, duke Elah, duke Pinon. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, taking the closing list together, simply set these names in their place among the chiefs of Edom, drawn from the older record. The witnesses read them in the light of the rule Charles Ellicott had laid down at the head of the list, that these are not personal names but tribal and local designations, the heads of the thousands or clans of Edom; so that Aholibamah, standing first here, is in truth a district that took its name, whether from the Horite woman who became Esau's wife or from the place itself, and Elah and Pinon likewise mark clans and their seats rather than single men.

The witnesses recall that the name Aholibamah has already crossed the chapter twice, once as one of Esau's wives, the daughter of Anah, and once in the omitted notice among the sons of Anah, so that its reappearance here among the dukes confirms Ellicott's point that these Edomite names travel between persons, clans, and places. What was a woman's name becomes a clan and a chieftaincy; the register is naming the settled divisions of Edom, the tribal lordships into which the land fell when its kings were gone.

So the witnesses read this verse as part of the orderly close of the Edomite record. Where the kings rose and passed one by one from different cities, the chiefs represent Edom in its settled tribal state, each duke the head of a clan rooted in its own district. The Chronicler numbers them faithfully, Aholibamah and Elah and Pinon among the rest, completing the account of the nation that sprang from Esau; and the very fullness of the list, the witnesses feel, is a testimony that God kept his word to make Esau a people, with princes and chiefs of his own. Yet it is a people outside the covenant, its lordships tribal and its glory passing; and the historian sets down its dukes only to have done with Edom, that he may turn at last to the tribes of Israel and the line of the promised King.
7:
The sons of Javan, Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim, carry Japheth's line into the coastlands and islands of the Mediterranean, the nearer edge of the Gentile world. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note here the counterpart to the variant of the preceding verse: where Gen. 10:4 reads Dodanim, the Chronicler's text has Rodanim, the opening letter again exchanged, and this time, they observe, it cannot with certainty be determined which of the two forms is the original. It is a small thing, and they let it stand as such, an honest note in the margin of a faithfully copied list rather than a wound in the text.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke turns once more to the Targum, which paraphrases the sons of Javan into a string of peoples and cities, Macedon and Tarsus among them, with a second reading that names Elishah, Dardania, Antioch, and others; the old interpreters, uncertain of the ground, multiplied their guesses at the nations that had sprung from this seafaring stock. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry keeps his brief word for the whole branch, that these were planted in the isles of the Gentiles, those western parts of the world, the countries of Europe, of which the historian says little because the Jews had hitherto had small dealings with them.

The witnesses find the verse chiefly instructive for what it shows of the Chronicler's care and the world's confusion. Elishah and Tarshish and Kittim would echo again through the prophets as names for distant maritime peoples, yet even the commentators who prize the exactness of the text confess that the precise boundaries of Javan's sons are lost. Henry's reflection covers them: at this day, he says, the nations are so mingled by the enlargement of dominion and the mixing of peoples that no one of them descends entire from any of these springs; only this we are sure of, that God has created of one blood all nations of men. So the sons of Javan close the short account of Japheth, and the historian, having gestured toward the Gentile isles, turns south to the darker and more entangled line of Ham.
9:
The sons of Cush, Seba, Havilah, Sabta, Raamah, and Sabtecha, with Raamah's sons Sheba and Dedan, spread the Hamite line across Arabia and the lands toward the Red Sea. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gathers the Targum's identifications, which render these names into a string of peoples, the Sindi and Hindi, the Semadaei, the Libyans and the Zingitae, with the Mauritanians reckoned among their descendants; the old paraphrast, uncertain of the ground, scattered the Cushite tribes widely over Africa and the East. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note in passing one of the small orthographic differences that mark the Chronicler's copy, the name Raamah being spelled here with a slight variation from the form it wears in the older table.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws attention to a feature that will recur and that guards the reader against a natural confusion: the names Sheba and Dedan, here set down as sons of Raamah in the Cushite line, appear again lower in the chapter among the descendants of Keturah (v.32). The same names, he explains, may have been common to different tribes settled in different regions, so that a Sheba of Cush and a Sheba of Abraham's later stock need not be one people. It is a caution against reading these registers as if every repeated name marked a single family, when in truth the ancient world carried its names, like its peoples, into many scattered homes.

The witnesses thus keep the verse from being a mere catalogue by drawing out both the reach and the tangle of the Cushite line. Ellicott and Clarke together show a stock spread from the African coasts to the Arabian trading tribes whose names, Sheba and Dedan, would sound again in the prophets as the far merchants of gold and spices. And the doubling of those names, noted so carefully, illustrates the historian's whole difficulty and his fidelity to it: he copies what he received, tribe after tribe, even where the same word serves two peoples, trusting his readers to distinguish, and reserving his closest attention for the single line that leads at last out of all this branching to Abraham.
10:
One name in the Hamite line breaks the pattern of bare succession, for of Nimrod alone something is told: Cush begat Nimrod, and he began to be mighty upon the earth. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that even here the Chronicler has abridged his source, keeping the single clause about Nimrod's greatness while dropping the fuller notice of Gen. 10:8-12, where his founding of a kingdom in Babel and his building of cities are related; the historian retains only the flash of the man's rising power and lets the rest go, as matter already known. The one clause he keeps, that Nimrod began to be mighty upon the earth, is thus made to carry the whole weight of the man's memory, the first stirring of empire in the world after the flood.

The witnesses read that retained clause with one voice as no compliment. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry takes Nimrod, the son of Cush, as one who began to be an oppressor, probably to the people of God in his time, the first of the great tyrants whose might was turned against the innocent. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke brings in the Targum, which glosses the very phrase to the same effect, that Nimrod began to be bold in sin, a murderer of the innocent, and a rebel before the Lord. So the being mighty upon the earth is read not as honest greatness but as violence and defiance, the earliest picture in the genealogy of power grown lawless.

The witnesses draw the moral that this one narrative gleam is set in the line of Ham on purpose. Where the sons of Japheth passed in a colourless list, here the historian pauses just long enough to mark the rise of the first oppressor, the founder of Babel, from whom the confusion of the nations would spread. Clarke and Henry agree that the notice, brief as it is, carries a warning: might that begins in rebellion against God, however it dazzles the world, stands under his judgment; and the Chronicler, who elsewhere gives only names, keeps this one deed because it shows the kingdom of man rising in pride against the kingdom of God, the very rivalry his whole book, tracing the throne of David toward the throne of Christ, is written to resolve.
11:
From Mizraim, the father of Egypt, springs a cluster of peoples, Ludim, Anamim, Lehabim, and Naphtuhim, and the witnesses mark this branch as one the historian records with special care. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had noted the reason in advance: because with the descendants of Mizraim the Israel of God had so severe a struggle to get out of the land of Egypt, the branches of Mizraim are particularly set down here, where lesser families are passed over. The Egyptians, and the tribes akin to them along the Nile and the Libyan coast, are named not for their own sake but because they would loom so large in the story of the bondage and the exodus. The Egyptians, chief among these tribes, would hold Israel in the house of bondage and then be broken for their sake, so that Henry's very reason for the fullness of the list, the severe struggle to come out of Egypt, presses upon every name in it.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke turns as before to the Targum, which renders these obscure Egyptian sub-peoples into names of its own, the Nivitaei, the Mariotaei, the Libakaei, and the Pentaskenaei; the paraphrast, having no sure knowledge of the tribes Mizraim begat, fitted them to such peoples of Egypt and its borders as he could. The identifications are uncertain, and the witnesses do not press them, but they show the persistent effort of the old interpreters to keep even the dimmest names of the table tied to some living nation.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the governing principle that keeps such names from being read as individuals: throughout this abridgment of the Table of Nations the proper names represent peoples and countries, the whole forming a classified summary of Hebrew geographical knowledge. Ludim and Naphtuhim, then, are districts and tribes of the Egyptian world, and their place in the register is Egypt's place in Israel's memory. The witnesses leave the verse as a reminder that the Chronicler's genealogy is also a map: he lingers over the sons of Mizraim because Egypt would be at once the house of bondage and the land God overthrew for his people's sake, so that even here the list is bending toward the great deliverance that lay ahead.
12:
The line of Mizraim runs on through Pathrusim and Casluhim to Caphthorim, and the verse carries the one parenthesis that lifts it above a mere list, the note that of these came the Philistines. The witnesses fix on this remark, for the Philistines would be among Israel's most persistent adversaries, and here the genealogy quietly traces their origin back into the Egyptian stock of Ham. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had explained why the branches of Mizraim are recorded with such fullness at all, that it was because of the long and severe dealings the people of God had with these southern nations; and the Philistines, sprung from this line, are a case in point, a people whose enmity would run from the days of the judges to the days of David whose throne the book is about to raise.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, following the Targum, offers the one identification the old paraphrast ventured for the last of these names, that the Caphthorim are the Cappadocians; a guess, like the others in this section, uncertain of its ground, yet witnessing to the effort to lodge each Egyptian sub-people in some known nation. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott's rule again governs the reading, that these are the names not of persons but of peoples and countries, so that Pathrusim marks the folk of Pathros in upper Egypt and Casluhim and Caphthorim their neighbouring tribes, districts of the Egyptian world rather than single ancestors.

The witnesses thus make the verse a small but pointed instance of the genealogy's design. Amid a run of shadowy Egyptian names the historian inserts the note about the Philistines, and the commentators seize it because it shows the register doing more than counting heads: it is quietly identifying the peoples whose collisions with Israel fill the later history. Where the earlier books tell of the Philistine wars, the Chronicler here reaches behind them to their fountain in Mizraim, so that a reader who knows the coming story feels, even in this dry parenthesis, the shadow of the enemy that David would at last subdue, and understands why the line of Ham is traced with so watchful an eye.
13:
The historian comes now to Canaan, and with him to the peoples whose land was pledged to Abraham's seed: Canaan begat Zidon his firstborn, and Heth. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the Targum's gloss on the firstborn, that Canaan begat Bothniam his first-born, who built Sidon, the paraphrast tying the name at once to the great Phoenician city on the coast. From Zidon would come the Sidonians, the seafaring Phoenicians of the north, and from Heth the Hittites, whose children Abraham would treat with for a burying-place; the two eldest sons of Canaan already name two of the peoples woven through Israel's history.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley pauses here to lay down the rule for all the names that follow to the end of the branch: those which come after are not the names of particular persons but of peoples and nations, all of them descended from Canaan, though some were afterward extinct, or confounded with others of their brethren by cohabitation and intermarriage, whereby they lost their names, which is why they are heard of no more. The observation reaches forward over the whole Canaanite list, teaching the reader not to look for individual men behind Jebusite or Amorite but for tribes that rose, mingled, and in time disappeared.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry keeps the theological stress that makes this branch matter. Canaan, from whom came the Canaanites, is a name of great note in the Jewish story, for with these descendants the Israel of God had severe struggles to get into the promised land; and therefore the sons of Canaan, like those of Mizraim, are recorded here with a fullness the historian denies to friendlier nations. The witnesses converge, then, on a single sense: the verse opens the roll of the seven nations of Canaan, the peoples Israel was to dispossess, and it opens it with Zidon and Heth, the coastland traders and the hill-country Hittites, so that the genealogy of Ham's youngest son becomes, in effect, a table of the inheritance God had marked out for his people and of the powers he would drive before them.
19:
To Eber were born two sons, and the naming of the elder carries a memory of one of the great turning-points of the primeval world: the name of the one was Peleg, and the name of his brother Joktan. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the Targum's explanation of both names, that the first son was called Peleg, that is, division, because in his days the inhabitants of the earth were divided according to their languages, and his brother Joktan because in his days the years of men began to be shortened on account of their iniquities. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gives the same sense of the elder name in a word: Peleg, divided, marking the age when mankind was divided in their languages and their habitations, the scattering that followed the confusion of tongues.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as the genealogy's quiet acknowledgment of Babel. In the very stem of Eber, from which the Hebrews sprang, a name is fixed to the moment the one human family broke into the many nations the table has just finished cataloguing, so that Peleg's name stands like a date-mark over the whole dispersion. The Targum's added note on Joktan, that in his days human life was shortened for men's sins, the witnesses report as tradition rather than doctrine, one of the paraphrast's attempts to read a meaning into every name, of the kind the commentators elsewhere treat with caution.

What the witnesses stress is the parting of the ways this verse records. From Joktan would spring the many Arabian tribes catalogued in the verse that follows, a whole spreading branch; but the line of promise runs through Peleg, and it is Peleg, not his more prolific brother, that the historian will trace onward toward Abraham. The two sons of Eber thus stand at a fork: one name gathers up the scattering of the nations and the founding of the desert tribes, the other carries the narrow covenant thread. The Chronicler names both, as his manner is, and then, faithful to his single purpose, keeps only Peleg, letting Joktan's crowded line be numbered once and set aside.
20:
Joktan's line is given in full, thirteen sons from Almodad and Sheleph to the last of the number, the founders of the Arabian tribes that spread through the southern desert. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke makes this verse the occasion for a long draught from the Targum, which fastens a fanciful etymology on name after name. Almodad, it says, divided and measured the earth by lines; Sheleph assigned the rivers to be boundaries; Hazarmaveth prepared a place of snares to kill travellers by the highways; and Jerah built inns, and when any came to eat and drink gave them deadly poison, and so seized their property.

Clarke retells these traditions with a dry relish and then reckons them up: according to the paraphrast the first two sons of Joktan were geographers, who measured the land and set the courses of its rivers, the third a public robber who laid ambushes on the roads, and the fourth an unprincipled innkeeper who poisoned his richer guests to inherit their goods. Such things, he adds with a glance at his own age, have been done even in modern times. The witnesses report the whole as a specimen of the Targum's method, weaving a story out of the sound of each name, and Clarke plainly offers it more for its curiosity and its moral than as sober history.

Beneath the entertainment the witnesses keep the verse's real place in the design. Joktan's thirteen sons are the eastern and Arabian peoples, a broad and vigorous branch of Eber's house, and their careful enumeration shows again the Chronicler's evenhandedness in numbering even the lines he means to set aside. For with all their spreading tribes, this is where Joktan's branch ends in the register; having counted the sons of the younger brother, the historian turns back to Peleg, through whom alone the line of promise runs. The colourful legends Clarke gathers only sharpen the contrast the witnesses feel throughout the chapter, between the many nations whose names are given once and left, and the single chosen family whose descent is followed link by link to Abraham and beyond.
32:
The register turns next to the sons of Keturah, and the witnesses begin by defending her honour. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes on the word the text uses of her, that she was Abraham's concubine, and insists it be rightly understood: the Hebrew term marks a wife of the second rank, so that Keturah was neither harlot nor concubine in the low sense our language attaches to the word, but a true wife taken after Sarah's death. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the list of her offspring, Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah, with the sons of Jokshan, Sheba and Dedan, is an extract from Gen. 25:1-4.

Ellicott then weighs several textual points with his usual care. Medan, he suggests, is very likely a mere repetition of Midian, the two names so alike that an ancient copyist may have doubled one into two. He observes that the older record adds the sons of Dedan, Asshurim, Letushim, and Leummim, which the Chronicler omits, and judges the omission deliberate rather than a defect of his text, since those three names are of a different form from the rest and may themselves be an interpolation. Of Midian he remarks that it is the most important of these tribes, the Midianites who wandered in the peninsula of Sinai; and he notes again that Sheba and Dedan reappear here after standing earlier among the sons of Cush (v.9), the same names borne by different tribes settled in different regions.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as both a record and a set of small puzzles honestly handled. Keturah's line, like Ishmael's, is a collateral branch of Abraham's house, the stock of the eastern and Arabian peoples, and it is numbered here to complete the account of the patriarch's children before the historian closes upon Isaac. Clarke's defence of Keturah rescues her from an unjust word, and Ellicott's notes on Medan and on the omitted Dedanite tribes show the commentators reasoning soberly about the state of the text rather than glossing over its difficulties. Together they leave the verse as a faithful register of Abraham's sons by his second wife, the children who, as the law had said, were sent away eastward with gifts while Isaac kept the inheritance of promise.
48:
The succession continues: when Samlah was dead, Shaul of Rehoboth by the river reigned in his stead. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the king's name is Saul, the very name that Israel's own first king would later bear, a small coincidence that sets the elder nation's monarchy beside the younger's. His city he takes to be Rehoboth by the river, probably the same as the Rehoboth of the old table of nations (Gen. 10:11), understood as the suburbs of Nineveh, the river being the Euphrates; so this Edomite king, if the identification hold, came from the far northeast, near the great Mesopotamian city.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke brings in the Targum, which names the place in its own fashion, calling it a great city built on the banks of the Euphrates; the paraphrast, like Ellicott, reaches toward the northern river to locate Shaul's home. The witnesses set the two readings side by side without forcing a decision, content that both point the same way, to a royal city on or near the Euphrates, far from the heart of Edom, one more sign of how widely the crown wandered in its search for a head. That the older record and the Targum alike reach so far to the north for Shaul's city, whether to the suburbs of Nineveh or to some great town upon the great river, only widens the picture of an Edom whose kings were drawn from the very ends of its world.

The witnesses find the verse quietly telling. That a king of Edom should bear the name Saul and hail from a city by the great river reminds the reader how the elder nation's kingship differed from Israel's: it was drawn from many places and many houses, without the settled succession that would mark, for good or ill, the thrones of Israel and Judah. Ellicott and Clarke together fix Shaul in his northern city, and the witnesses leave him there as the sixth in a line of kings none of whom passed his crown to a son. The name Saul, sounding here in Edom before it sounded in Israel, only deepens the sense of contrast that governs the passage, the shadow-monarchy of the rejected brother running ahead in time yet lacking the promise that would one day make the true king's throne endure.
50:
The last of the kings is reached, and for the first time a queen is named beside him: when Baalhanan was dead, Hadad reigned in his stead, his city was Pai, and his wife's name was Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Mezahab. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott handles the several textual points with care. Some manuscripts, he notes, add son of Achbor to Baalhanan, as the older record does; the name Pai is written Pau in many copies and in Gen. 36:39, which he judges the right reading; and where the parallel gives the king's name as Hadar, that too is probably a mistake for Hadad. Of the queen he observes that Mehetabel means God benefits, and that she may perhaps have been an Israelite, since no other queen of Edom is named, though her own name is Aramean rather than Hebrew.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the name of the queen's grandmother, Mezahab, which he reads as meaning the golden waters, or literally, what is gold. He reports the Targum's fanciful expansion, that Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, was so earnest and diligent in business that she grew immensely rich, but that upon her conversion she said, What is this silver, and what is this gold? that is, they are of no real worth. Clarke offers the paraphrase for its curiosity and its moral rather than as history, one more instance of the old interpreters spinning a lesson out of the meaning of a name.

The witnesses read this closing royal notice as the end of Edom's monarchy and, in Clarke's Targum, an unexpected sermon. That the record pauses to name a queen and to trace her descent through Matred and Mezahab is itself remarkable in so terse a list, and Ellicott's guess that Mehetabel may have been an Israelite, or at least an Aramean, quietly ties even the last Edomite throne to the wider family of nations. Clarke's Targumic legend, turning the golden-named grandmother into a text on the vanity of riches, the witnesses take as tradition, not doctrine, of the sort they elsewhere handle with reserve. But the substance is plain: with this Hadad the line of kings ends, and the historian, having named the last of them and his queen, is ready to record how the crown of Edom gave way to its dukes.
51:
The monarchy of Edom closes and gives way to a rule of chiefs: Hadad died also, and the dukes of Edom were duke Timnah, duke Aliah, duke Jetheth. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the transition closely. Better rendered, he says, the verse runs, And Hadad died, and there arose chiefs of Edom, which states that Hadad was the last king and that after his death the country was governed by the heads of its several clans, without any central authority. The older record lacks the words And Hadad died, passing straight from the kings to the chiefs; and the title itself, he explains, means the heads of the thousands or clans of Edom, so that these are not personal names but tribal and local designations, Timnah and Aholibamah among them being in truth towns, as the close of the account in the law makes clear.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gives the Targum's account of the same collapse, that Hadad died and his kingdom ended, for his land was subdued and the dukes of Edom ruled thereafter in the region of Gebal; the paraphrast too marks the passing of the crown into the hands of the clan-chiefs. Ellicott adds the conjecture of Ewald, that the chiefs are set after the kings as though David had already vanquished the last king of Edom and reduced the country to a merely tribal government under himself; and he notes that a Hadad who fled as a young man to Egypt at David's conquest (1 Kings 11:14-22) may have been a grandson of Hadad the last king.

The witnesses find in this verse the fitting end of the whole Edomite record and a pointed turn toward the chapter's true subject. Edom, which had run ahead of Israel into monarchy, now falls back out of it, its kings giving way to a scattering of clan-chiefs, its unity dissolved, and, if Ewald and Ellicott are right, its last king overthrown by the very David whose house the Chronicler is tracing. The king of Edom, so often named elsewhere in the sacred history (Num. 20:14), passes here into a memory, and the throne of Esau's line proves as impermanent as its succession was disordered. So the long parenthesis of Edom ends where it must, in the failure of its kingship, and the reader is made ready for the enduring kingdom of the younger brother that the rest of the book will unfold.
4:
With Noah and his three sons the record reaches the second head of the human race, the man in whom the line survived the flood, and the witnesses pause here before the great fan of the nations opens. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes up the three names and their meanings: there is little doubt that Ham means black or sunburnt, that Japheth is probably the fair-skinned, and that Shem has been compared with an Assyrian word meaning brownish; so the three names seem to allude to differences of racial complexion in the peoples that would spring from them. He notes that the whole list from here through the sons of Shem is an abridgment of the Table of Nations in Gen. 10, and that the proper names in it represent not persons but peoples and countries, so that the older Jewish interpreters, adding them all together, reckoned the world at seventy nations, a classified summary of the whole ethnical and geographical knowledge of Hebrew antiquity.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the verse as the pivot of the chapter's design, where the historian repeats the replenishing of the earth by the sons of Noah after the flood. He arranges the three lines by their bearing on the church: he begins with Japheth, whose sons were strangers, planted in the isles of the Gentiles; he proceeds to Ham, many of whose sons became enemies; and he ends with Shem, the ancestors and allies of the covenant people. Yet Henry will not let the branching lines obscure the common root, pressing home that God has made of one blood all nations of men, all descended from one Adam and one Noah, so that we may ask with the prophet, Have we not all one father? Has not one God created us? (Mal. 2:10).

So the witnesses treat this hinge-verse with more weight than its three familiar names might suggest. Ellicott's philology and Henry's theology converge on a single point: that the table which follows is no idle roll of forgotten tribes but the deliberate spreading-out of humanity from Noah, arranged so that, having shown the whole world descended from one man, the Chronicler may narrow again to the one nation, and within it the one family, through which the blessing was to come to all the rest. Noah, the father of the post-flood world, stands here as the second Adam of the genealogy, and his three sons are the fountains from which, in the verses that follow, the seventy nations will pour.
31:
Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah close the roll of Ishmael's sons, and the text sets its own seal on the number: these are the sons of Ishmael. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies the first of the three, Jetur, with the Itureans who dwelt beyond Jordan, the people whose region is named among the tetrarchies at the opening of the Gospel history (Luke 3:1); of the other two he says frankly that the names are obscure, a candour the witnesses respect, for not every ancient tribe can be traced. With this verse the twelve princes stand complete, the tale of Ishmael's house fully told.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, treating the whole block together, note that these generations of Ishmael are taken over from the older record and there explained, the Chronicler adding nothing but faithfully preserving the count. The witnesses read the closing formula, these are the sons of Ishmael, as the historian's way of ruling a line under the fulfilled promise: twelve princes were pledged, and twelve are named, from Nebaioth the firstborn to Kedemah the last, and the reckoning is done.

The witnesses find in that completeness a quiet testimony to the faithfulness of God even in the lines he does not choose. The Itureans beyond Jordan, still known by their tribal name in the days of the Gospel, and the other Ishmaelite tribes scattered through the desert, are the living proof that the word spoken to Abraham did not fail; and the Chronicler honours it by numbering them to the full before he leaves them. Yet the very finality of the notice marks the parting. Having closed the account of Ishmael, the historian is free to return to the son of promise; the desert princes have their place in the record and no more, and the line of blessing, which never ran through them, is about to be traced afresh through Keturah's sons and then, past them all, to Isaac. Jetur especially, surviving as the Itureans into the age of the Gospel, shows how long these desert names endured, a tribe still known by its founder's name generations after Ishmael, and yet numbered here only to be honourably dismissed with the rest. The completeness of Ishmael's list is thus both an honouring of the promise and a signal that the register's true business lies elsewhere.
33:
The sons of Midian, Ephah, Epher, Henoch, Abida, and Eldaah, are numbered, and the text closes the branch with its summary: all these are the sons of Keturah. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott counts them as the five clans or tribes of Midian, and observes that these, taken with the seven names already given, make up a total of twelve tribes for Keturah, so that Abraham's second wife, like his handmaid's son Ishmael, becomes the mother of twelve peoples. Of the first of them, Ephah, he notes that the name appears in the Assyrian records under the form Hayapa, among the tribes known to Tiglath-pileser, a token that these desert clans were real peoples still traceable in the annals of the great kings.

The witnesses mark the symmetry Ellicott draws out, for it is not accidental. Ishmael begot twelve princes, and Keturah's line is here reckoned at twelve tribes as well, the two collateral branches of Abraham's house each spreading into a round number of peoples across Arabia and the East. The Midianites, chief among Keturah's descendants, would cross Israel's path again and again, as the people among whom Moses found refuge and took a wife, and as the raiders whom Gideon would rout; here they stand at their fountainhead, five clans sprung from Midian the son of Abraham's old age.

Yet the witnesses feel, as with Ishmael's list, the deliberate finality of the closing words. All these are the sons of Keturah: the branch is complete, and complete means concluded. The historian has honoured Abraham's other children by numbering them fully, the eastern and Arabian tribes descended from his second wife, but the covenant did not run through them. Having counted Keturah's twelve as he counted Ishmael's, the Chronicler has now cleared away every son of Abraham but one, and the reader knows the next word must be the return to Isaac. The genealogy's method is again on plain display: the collateral lines are named generously and then ruled off, so that the single line of promise may stand out the more clearly against the many peoples that sprang, like it, from Abraham but not, like it, toward Christ.
34:
With a single verse the historian gathers up all that has gone before and sets the stage for all that follows: Abraham begat Isaac; the sons of Isaac, Esau and Israel. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the words are drawn from Gen. 25:19, and that the fresh mention of Abraham begetting Isaac, though Isaac was named already, marks the true resumption of the chosen line after the collateral branches of Ishmael and Keturah have been dismissed. He explains the order of the two sons: Esau is named first, not because he was the elder, though he was, but because the tribes of Esau are to be enumerated first, so that the register may deal with the rejected line and clear it away before settling upon the chosen.

Ellicott lingers on the second name, Israel. It is the more honourable appellation, the name given at Peniel (Gen. 32:28), and it so far supplanted Jacob that Israel became the standing name of the chosen people, Jacob surviving chiefly in poetry and prophecy. He notes too that some modern scholars have seen in such double names as Jacob-Israel and Esau-Edom a trace of an ancient fusion of distinct races, though he reports the theory without adopting it. The witnesses take the naming of Israel here as the quiet signal that the genealogy has at last reached the people for whose sake the whole book is written.

The verse is, in the witnesses' reading, the great fork of the chapter. Two sons of Isaac, two nations, stand side by side: Esau, from whom Edom would spring, and Israel, in whom the covenant was lodged. The historian, true to the method he has followed since the flood, will take up the rejected brother first, numbering Esau's sons and the kings and dukes of Edom, and then set them all aside; but the reader already knows where the line runs. By calling the younger son not Jacob but Israel, the Chronicler names the nation before he narrows to it, and the coming roll of Edomite chiefs is felt from the first as a long parenthesis before the register returns, in the chapters that follow, to trace the tribes of Israel and the house of David toward the throne on which the whole book is bent.
41:
The Horite line runs on through Anah to his son Dishon and the sons of Dishon, Amram, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott pauses over the terse phrase the sons of Anah, Dishon, and defends it against the charge of defect. In the older record, he notes, Gen. 36:25 adds after Dishon the name of Aholibamah the daughter of Anah, whom the Chronicler here omits; but Dishon, like Ammon or Israel, is the collective name of a whole group of tribes or clans, so that there is nothing strange in the expression the sons of Anah, Dishon, a single tribal name standing for a numerous posterity.

Ellicott draws a thread forward from the omitted name. That same Aholibamah, the daughter of Anah, though passed over here in the list of Seir's descendants, reappears at the chapter's close among the chiefs of Edom, where a duke, or rather a district, bears her name (v.52). The witnesses take the point as a caution and a key: these Edomite and Horite names are as much tribal and territorial as personal, so that a woman named among the Horites may leave her name upon a clan or a place counted afterward among the dukes. The Chronicler's omission of Aholibamah here is thus no loss, for her name is not lost from the record but only carried to its proper place in the list of chiefs.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, surveying the Horite genealogy as a whole, keep the reader mindful that these clans of Seir are set down because they were fused with the house of Esau into the one people of Edom, their princes numbered alongside his. The witnesses read this verse, then, as a further link in that absorbed and intermarried line, and find in Ellicott's handling of it a model of the sober judgment the chapter demands: a phrase that looks abrupt is explained by the collective force of the names, an omission is answered by a later appearance, and the whole tangled register of Horite and Edomite clans is left standing as a faithful account of the mingled stock that possessed Mount Seir before ever a king reigned there.
46:
The line of kings reaches a warrior: when Husham was dead, Hadad the son of Bedad, which smote Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his stead, and his city was Avith. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott seizes first on the deed, a glimpse, he says, of the restless feuds that prevailed from time immemorial between these neighbouring tribes and peoples of kindred origin; and he notes that, like the judges of Israel, the kings of Edom seem to have been raised to their place by special emergencies, this Hadad rising, it may be, on the strength of his victory over Midian. The field of Moab he takes to mean the open country, and of Avith he says only that, like the other royal cities of this list, it is unknown beyond this passage, remarking that its Hebrew spelling here differs from the older text by a simple transposition of two letters.

Ellicott lingers longest over the king's name, and through it opens the religion of Edom. Hadad, he explains, is the name of a Syrian deity, a form of the sun-god, the same divine title that appears in the royal names Ben-hadad and Hadadezer; and he traces its worship through the cry of the vintagers, suggesting that Hadad, like Bacchus, was regarded as the giver of the grapes, a god of the vine whose name the harvesters shouted (Isa. 16:9-10). That an Edomite king should bear the name of a heathen god is, for the witnesses, a window into the polytheism of ancient Edom, a nation kindred to Israel by blood yet given over to the gods of the surrounding peoples.

The witnesses thus find this verse richer than the bare succession it records. Where most of the kings pass with only a name and a city, Hadad the son of Bedad is marked by a battle and by a name freighted with idolatry, and Ellicott draws out both. The war with Midian shows the Edomite throne, like the seat of Israel's judges, thrown up by the emergencies of a violent age rather than handed down in peace; and the name Hadad, the sun-god and the giver of grapes, shows the elder brother's nation worshipping the very deities Israel was forbidden. So even this brief royal notice serves the chapter's undertone, setting the god-named, war-raised kings of Edom against the covenant people whose God was the LORD, and whose true and lasting king was yet to come.
47:
The crown passes again to a new house and a new town: when Hadad was dead, Samlah of Masrekah reigned in his stead. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott offers the one gloss the name invites, that Masrekah means the place of Sorek vines, the choice red grapes of the ancient world; the king's city was named for its vineyards, a small notice that fits the picture, drawn out at the previous king, of a land whose very gods and towns were bound up with the vine. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, numbering the succession as a whole, place Samlah in his order among the kings whose names answer to the older record, the fifth in the Edomite line.

The witnesses note again the mark that runs through the whole list, that the throne moves from stranger to stranger. Samlah is of Masrekah, as Hadad before him was of Avith and Jobab of Bozrah; no son follows his father, but each king rises from a different place, so that the reader sees, verse by verse, a monarchy that never settled into a house. Ellicott's remark that these royal names are as much tribal and local as personal governs the reading of Samlah too: behind the man stands his town, and the crown attaches to no lasting line but wanders from clan to clan across the land of Edom.

The witnesses draw from this the same quiet lesson the chapter has been teaching. Edom, the elder nation, had kings while Israel had none, and its vineyards and cities gave them their names; yet the very shape of its kingship, restless, elective, passing from Masrekah to the next man's town, betrays a throne without promise behind it. The Chronicler records Samlah of Masrekah faithfully, as he records them all, but the roll of ever-changing royal cities only sharpens the contrast his book is built upon. For while Edom's crown drifted from house to house, God was preparing in the younger nation a throne that would not wander, the house of David, to which the whole genealogy is bending, and in which the kingdom would at last be made sure for ever.
49:
The line runs on: when Shaul was dead, Baalhanan the son of Achbor reigned in his stead. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott turns, as with Hadad, to what the royal name confesses. Baal-hanan, he explains, means Baal has been gracious, or Baal bestowed, formed like the name Johanan, which means the LORD has been gracious, and like Hananiah and even the Punic Hannibal; and he judges that this name, together with the name Hadad borne by two of these kings, plainly indicates the polytheism of ancient Edom. The very crowns of the elder nation were inscribed, so to speak, with the names of its gods, Baal and Hadad, the deities of the surrounding heathen.

The witnesses take the point as more than antiquarian. Where Israel's names so often bound a child to the LORD, Baalhanan of Edom bore in his own name the confession that Baal had shown him favour; the contrast between the two nations, kindred in blood, is written into their very naming. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, counting the succession, set Baalhanan the son of Achbor in his place as the seventh of the Edomite kings, his name answering to the older record; and the witnesses read him, with Ellicott, as a standing token of the idolatry that filled the land of Esau.

So the witnesses find in this brief notice the same undertone that has run through the roll of kings. Edom's throne, restless and elective, is now shown to be idolatrous as well, its rulers named for Baal and for the sun-god, worshippers of the powers Israel was called to renounce. The Chronicler records Baalhanan without comment, as he records them all, but the accumulation, king after king rising from a different city and bearing the name of a heathen god, tells its own story.
