It sets in relief the difference the whole book is written to display: that while the elder brother's nation multiplied kings devoted to Baal, God was raising up in the younger a house devoted, however imperfectly, to himself, and preparing through it the King in whom every knee should one day bow to the LORD alone.
54:
The register of Edom ends with its last two chiefs, duke Magdiel and duke Iram, and the summary, these are the dukes of Edom. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott counts the names and finds them short of the expected number: only eleven chiefs are given here, whereas the older record reckons twelve, or thirteen (Gen. 36:15-19), so that a name may well have fallen out of the ancient text from which the Chronicler drew his list. The witnesses take the note as one more instance of the sober textual attention the whole chapter has required, the commentators neither hiding the shortfall nor magnifying it, but marking it as the likely loss of a single name in the long course of copying.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley closes the genealogy with a meditation that gathers up the whole. Let us, he says, in reading these lists, think of the multitudes that have gone through the world, have successively acted their parts in it, and retired into darkness. All these, and all theirs, had their day; many of them made a mighty noise in the world, until their day came to fall and their place knew them no more. The paths of death are trodden paths; and he turns the reflection upon the reader with a searching question, how soon are we to tread them.

The witnesses let Wesley's word stand as the fitting close to a chapter of names. All the fathers and nations and kings and dukes the register has numbered, from Adam at its head to Iram at its foot, have passed off the stage of the world and left only their names behind; and the whole long roll becomes, in the end, a memento of mortality, a reminder that the generations rise and make their noise and sink again into silence. Yet the genealogy is not despair but hope, for its very purpose has been to keep unbroken, through all this passing of the peoples, the single line that runs toward the King who does not die. Ellicott's care for a lost name and Wesley's care for the passing soul meet in the same reverence for a record that, counting the dead, is in truth tracing the way to life, and preparing the reader, now that Edom is done, for the tribes of Israel and the throne of David that the rest of the book will raise.
8:
With the sons of Ham, Cush, Mizraim, Put, and Canaan, the historian turns from the strangers of the north to the peoples of the south, and the witnesses note at once that this is the line most bound up with Israel's later story. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott had already given the name its sense, that Ham means black or sunburnt, fitting the hot southern lands into which these families moved; and he reminds us that here, as through the whole table, the names stand for peoples and countries rather than for single men, so that Cush and Mizraim and Canaan are nations in embryo.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the branch with an eye fixed on the church's enemies. The sons of Ham, he says, moved southward toward Africa and those parts of Asia that lay that way, and many of them became foes to the people of God. From Cush would come Nimrod the oppressor; from Mizraim the Egyptians, and from Canaan the Canaanites, two names of great note in the story of Israel, for with their descendants the Israel of God had severe struggles, first to get out of the land of Egypt and then to get into the land of Canaan. It is on this account, Henry observes, that the branches of Mizraim and of Canaan are afterward recorded in such detail, while the other families are passed over quickly. And he sets against their enmity the measure of God's love for his own, who valued Israel so highly that he gave Egypt for their ransom (Isa. 43:3) and cast out all these nations before them.

So the witnesses make this verse the threshold of a line the reader is meant to watch. The four sons of Ham are the fountains of the peoples that would ring Israel about, the Cushites, the Egyptians, the Libyans of Put, and above all the Canaanites whose land was promised to Abraham's seed. Henry's arrangement, drawn from the historian's own proportions, teaches that the Chronicler lingers over these hostile branches not out of interest in their greatness but because the church's God would be glorified in delivering his people out of their hands, so that even a list of Ham's sons becomes a quiet preface to the exodus and the conquest.
14:
The roll of Canaan's sons continues with the Jebusite, the Amorite, and the Girgashite, three of the peoples whose names the reader of the law knows as the standing list of the nations to be driven out. The witnesses read them exactly as the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley had directed at the head of the branch, not as the names of particular persons but of peoples and nations, all descended from Canaan; some of them, he adds, were afterward extinct, or so confounded with their brethren by intermarriage that they lost their distinct names, which is the reason several are never mentioned again. The Jebusite, the Amorite, and the Girgashite are tribes, not men, and their place in the genealogy is their place in the land Israel was to enter.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the branch its weight in the covenant story. These are among the Canaanites with whom, he says, the Israel of God had such severe struggles to possess the promised land, and it is for that reason the sons of Canaan are recorded so particularly, where the historian passes lightly over nations that never touched the church. Each name in this verse would recur in the commands and the histories of the conquest: the Amorite of the hill country, the Jebusite who held Jerusalem until David took it, the Girgashite numbered among the seven nations God engaged to cast out.

The witnesses thus keep even this dense line of tribal names from lying inert. Wesley's caution and Henry's application together turn the verse into a map of the coming inheritance and a lesson in providence. The peoples here named filled the land of promise, and their genealogical descent from Canaan, the son under the ancient shadow, marks them as the very nations whose iniquity would in time be full and whose place Israel would take. That some of them, as Wesley notes, faded and were absorbed and heard of no more, while the seed of Abraham was preserved and multiplied, is itself a silent testimony to the God who keeps his covenant line unbroken while the nations rise and mingle and pass away.
17:
The historian comes at last to the line he has been moving toward, the sons of Shem, Elam, Asshur, Arphaxad, Lud, and Aram, with Uz, Hul, Gether, and Meshech. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley notes a grammatical nicety in the way they are strung together: the last four names are not, strictly, sons of Shem at all, but sons of Aram, so that either the word sons is here taken broadly to include grandsons, or the phrase the children of Aram must be understood before Uz, as it stands expressed in Gen. 10:23. The Chronicler, abridging, has listed Aram's sons directly under Shem, and Wesley supplies the clause his brevity omitted, so that the reader who compares the two lists is not to think the historian has confused Aram's sons with Shem's, but only compressed them, trusting the fuller order of the law to set the descent straight.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marks this branch as the one that most concerns the covenant, the ancestors and allies of the church. These, he says, peopled Asia and spread themselves eastward, and from them descended the Assyrians, the Syrians, the Chaldeans, the Persians, and the Arabians. Elam stands at the head of the Elamites and Persians, Asshur of the Assyrians, Aram of the Syrians or Arameans, while Arphaxad, set quietly among his brothers, is the one whose line the historian will presently follow to the exclusion of all the rest, for from Arphaxad, through Eber, the promise ran toward Abraham.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott keeps before us that these too are peoples as much as men, the same rule that governs the whole table; the sons of Shem are the nations of the nearer East, the classified summary of a world the Hebrews knew from close and often bitter acquaintance. The witnesses agree that the verse is a turning-point in the register: having spread out Japheth to the isles and Ham to the south, the Chronicler now names the eastern stock from which his own nation sprang, and among all these founders of empires it is the unremarkable Arphaxad who carries the sacred line. The great nations, Assyria and Persia and Syria, are named and left; the thread of promise slips past them and holds to the family that would give the world its Redeemer.
18:
The register now abandons the many for the one, following Shem's line through a single channel: Arphaxad begat Shelah, and Shelah begat Eber. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley marks a well-known difficulty at the first link, that the word begat may be taken either immediately or mediately; for the Greek text and the genealogy of Luke 3:35 insert a second Cainan between Arphaxad and Shelah, so that Arphaxad may have begotten Shelah directly, or through that intervening son whom the Chronicler, like the Hebrew of the law, passes over. Wesley leaves the reader to weigh the two accounts, content that the line itself is sure whether the extra name be reckoned in or not.

The name that crowns the verse is Eber, and the witnesses feel its weight, for in Eber the historian reaches the eponym of the Hebrews themselves. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, tracing the descent of the promise, marks how it was translated from Shem to Eber and so to the Hebrew nation, entrusted above all peoples with that sacred treasure until the Messiah came. Everything in the surrounding table has been narrowing to this: out of the seventy nations the writer has kept only the sons of Shem, out of Shem's sons only Arphaxad, and now out of Arphaxad's line the single stem that runs through Shelah to Eber, in whom the covenant people find their very name.

The grammatical-historical witnesses read the verse as a study in the Chronicler's method of selection, keeping only the names that stand in the line of promise and letting the collateral branches fall away. Here the genealogy is at its most austere, three names and two acts of begetting, yet the witnesses find in that austerity the whole point: the historian is not writing the history of mankind but tracing a single thread through it, and each name he keeps, Arphaxad, Shelah, Eber, is kept because the blessing passed through it. So a verse that seems only a link in a chain is, in the witnesses' reading, the deliberate stripping-away of all the world but the one family through which God would keep his word to the fathers.
24:
Here the register turns back on itself and begins again, and the witnesses recognize the deliberate movement: Shem, Arphaxad, Shelah. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley states the design exactly. Having given a brief and general account of the original of the world and of the people in it, the historian now returns to a more large and particular account of the genealogy of Shem, from whom the Jews were descended. The wide survey of the nations is finished; the writer resumes the single line, naming Shem and Arphaxad and Shelah afresh, not to repeat himself idly but to pick up the one thread out of the whole spread cloth and follow it, this time, all the way to Abraham.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry describes the same turn as the register hastening to the line of Abraham, breaking off abruptly from all the other families of the sons of Noah but that of Arphaxad, from whom Christ was to come. The great promise of the Messiah, he says with Bishop Patrick, was translated from Adam to Seth, from Seth to Shem, from Shem to Eber, and so onward to the Hebrew nation, entrusted above all peoples with that sacred treasure. This verse is the hinge on which that narrowing turns: everything Japhetic and Hamite, and even most of Shem, is now left behind, and the historian sets his face wholly toward the chosen stem.

The witnesses find the repetition itself full of meaning. To name Shem and Arphaxad and Shelah a second time is to declare that the sprawling table of nations was only the setting for this one line, the frame around the picture. The Chronicler could have moved straight from Eber to Abraham, but by returning to Shem he shows the reader consciously stepping back from the whole world to trace the covenant descent alone, so that the resumed genealogy reads almost as a fresh beginning, the genealogy of promise lifted clear of the genealogy of the peoples. From this point the names will no longer be nations spreading outward but fathers narrowing inward, generation by generation, toward the man with whom God would make his covenant.
